items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: How do insulin and glucagon work together to regulate blood glucose?
items.correct: They are antagonistic — insulin lowers blood glucose (cells take up glucose, store as glycogen); glucagon raises it (glycogen broken back down to glucose).
items.misconception: Insulin and glucagon both lower blood glucose using different mechanisms.
items.random_wrong: Insulin works on muscles and glucagon works on the brain.
items.why: Students don't grasp 'antagonistic hormones' — they often think both hormones do similar jobs because both are about glucose.

items.q: What does the 'all-or-nothing' principle mean for action potentials?
items.correct: Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full size — sub-threshold stimuli produce no action potential.
items.misconception: Stronger stimuli produce larger action potentials.
items.random_wrong: Action potentials get bigger as they travel along the axon.
items.why: Students think 'more stimulus = bigger response' (analogous to volume control), missing that stimulus strength is coded by FREQUENCY of action potentials, not amplitude.
The answer examiners want is: Pulling. “Pushing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When you bend your arm, the biceps…
items.why: It's Contracts. “Relaxes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a biceps lifts 4 kg and you have 2 arms, total mass lifted?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
